1: The Philistines fought against Israel, and the men of Israel fled before the Philistines and fell on Mount Gilboa. The battle of Gilboa is the hinge of 1 Chronicles: the genealogical prologue (1 Chronicles 1–9) has established who Israel is; now the narrative begins with the death of the first king and the end of the old order. The pattern of defeat-and-restoration that governs the Chronicler’s theology is here at its starkest: Israel in full flight before their enemies, the king about to die. The old order’s collapse creates the space for the new — as Christ’s death precedes resurrection, as Israel’s exile precedes restoration.
2: The Philistines overtook Saul and his sons; and the Philistines struck down Jonathan and Abinadab and Malchishua, the sons of Saul. The three sons who die with their father complete the end of the Saulide royal line (Esh-baal/Ishbosheth survives but his brief contested reign is entirely omitted by the Chronicler). The death of Jonathan — David’s covenant friend, the loyal son of the disloyal king — is especially poignant. Jonathan, who consistently chose David over his own dynastic interest (1 Samuel 18:4; 1 Chronicles 23:17), perishes with the house that refused to acknowledge what Jonathan knew: YHWH had chosen David.
3: The battle pressed hard against Saul, and the archers found him, and he was wounded by the archers. Saul is brought down not by a warrior in hand-to-hand combat but by archers — by distant, impersonal projectiles. He cannot even die in the kind of battle he was supposed to fight. The contrast with David’s single combat against Goliath is exact: David trusted YHWH and fought decisively; Saul trusted in military formation and was helplessly picked off from afar. The king who would not trust YHWH in life cannot be rescued by human means in death.
4: Saul said to his armor-bearer, “Draw your sword and thrust me through with it, lest these uncircumcised come and mistreat me.” But his armor-bearer would not, for he feared greatly. So Saul took his own sword and fell upon it. Saul’s final act is self-destruction — the logical terminus of the trajectory of self-reliance that began with his first disobedience (1 Samuel 13:8-14). He dies by his own sword, the weapon that could not save him from uncircumcised enemies. The contrast with Christ is absolute: where Saul chose death to escape humiliation, Jesus endured humiliation and suffering to the end, “who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame” (Hebrews 12:2).
5: When his armor-bearer saw that Saul was dead, he also fell upon his sword and died. Saul’s death becomes the occasion of another death — the loyal servant killed not by the enemy but by the despair of his lord’s defeat. The armor-bearer’s suicide is the collateral tragedy of a kingdom built on the wrong foundation. The contrast with the disciples’ response to Christ’s death — initial despair that becomes resurrection hope — marks the difference between a death that only brings more death and the death of the Son of God that brings life (John 12:24).
6: Thus Saul died; he and his three sons and all his house died together. The Chronicler adds “and all his house” — a phrase that goes beyond 1 Samuel 31:6. The complete end of the Saulide royal household (in its fighting male members) is presented as the end of an era. The old order must completely end for the new to begin: as the old creation must pass away for the new creation to come (Revelation 21:1; 2 Corinthians 5:17), as the old covenant must be superseded by the new (Hebrews 8:13), so the Saulide dynasty must completely fall for the Davidic kingdom to arise.
7: When all the men of Israel who were in the valley saw that they had fled and that Saul and his sons were dead, they abandoned their cities and fled, and the Philistines came and lived in them. The scattering of the sheep when the shepherd falls echoes Jesus’ quotation of Zechariah 13:7 at Gethsemane: “I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered” (Matthew 26:31). As Israel’s defeat and scattering at Gilboa preceded the gathering under David, the disciples’ scattering at the cross preceded their gathering at Pentecost. The dispersion is not the end of the story.
8: The next day the Philistines came to strip the slain and found Saul and his sons fallen on Mount Gilboa. The despoiling of the fallen king’s body — stripped of armor, trophy-beheaded in v9 — is the Philistines’ moment of apparent triumph. The image of an anointed king despoiled by enemies runs forward to the cross: the soldiers divided Jesus’ garments and cast lots (Matthew 27:35; Psalms 22:18). Yet as David restored Israel’s honor after the Goliath battle reversed the stripping of the fallen, so Christ’s resurrection reversed the apparent victory of his enemies.
9: They stripped him and took his head and his armor, and sent messengers throughout the land of the Philistines to carry the good news to their idols and to the people. The Philistines’ proclamation of “good news” (ûlᵉḇaśśēr) to their idols is a grotesque parody of the true bᵉśōrāh (good news/gospel) — the announcement of victory in God’s name. The false gospel of Saul’s defeat is answered by the true gospel of David’s anointing — and ultimately by the true euangelion of Christ’s resurrection, the announcement of the true King’s victory over death, declared not to idols but to all the nations (Matthew 28:18-20).
10: They put his armor in the house of their gods and fastened his head in the house of Dagon. Saul’s armor placed in an idol’s temple repeats the very indignity that Saul himself had inflicted — he had put Goliath’s armor in his own tent (1 Samuel 17:54) and now receives the same treatment from his enemies. The ironic reversal is characteristic of Chronicler theology: the proud are humbled, the disobedient receive what they gave. Yet Dagon himself had already been humiliated before the ark (1 Samuel 5:1-7) — the idol that now receives Saul’s armor is the god already proven powerless before YHWH.
11: When all Jabesh-gilead heard all that the Philistines had done to Saul, all the valiant men arose and took away the body of Saul and the bodies of his sons, and brought them to Jabesh. The men of Jabesh-gilead act in covenant faithfulness — remembering Saul’s rescue of their city (1 Sam 11) by giving his body proper burial. Their act of loyal love for the fallen king is a type of the faithful disciples who took care of Jesus’ body (John 19:38-42; Mark 15:46) — acts of covenant devotion performed when hope seemed lost, which set the stage for the resurrection.
12: They buried their bones under the oak in Jabesh and fasted seven days. The seven-day fast — a full week of mourning — is the maximum mourning period (cf. Genesis 50:10). The entire community participates in lamenting the fallen king. Even the failed king receives appropriate mourning: his dignity as YHWH’s anointed is not erased by his failure. David himself would mourn Saul and Jonathan extravagantly (2 Samuel 1:11-12). The respect for the dead king’s body models the principle that even the broken and failed image-bearers of God retain their dignity — a dignity that the resurrection of the body (1 Corinthians 15:42-44) ultimately vindicates.
13: “So Saul died for his unfaithfulness (maʿal) that he committed against the LORD, in that he did not keep the word of the LORD.” The Chronicler’s theological verdict — unique to 1 Chronicles, absent from 1 Sam 31 — names the root cause: maʿal, the key Chronistic term for covenant treachery (29x in Chronicles). Saul’s maʿal consisted of consulting the medium at Endor (1 Sam 28) rather than seeking YHWH. The wages of covenant unfaithfulness is death (Romans 6:23) — and Saul’s death is presented not as bad luck in battle but as the inevitable consequence of abandoning the one who had established him.
14: “He did not seek (dāraš) the LORD; therefore he put him to death and turned the kingdom over to David son of Jesse.” The Chronicler’s final verdict pairs the negative (lōʾ dāraš — did not seek) with the positive transfer: YHWH himself executed the judgment and effected the succession. Dāraš YHWH (seeking/inquiring of the LORD) is the Chronicler’s defining criterion for legitimate kingship — every king is evaluated by whether he sought YHWH. David is the king who sought YHWH; Solomon is charged to seek YHWH (1 Chronicles 28:9); Asa, Jehoshaphat, and Hezekiah are commended for seeking YHWH; Saul is the fundamental negative example. Christ is the ultimate “seeker” of the Father — the one who does only what he sees the Father doing (John 5:19), who “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Hebrews 5:8), and who at the cross cried “My God, my God” (Psalms 22:1) — maintaining covenant relationship even in the darkness of abandonment. The kingdom that Saul forfeited by not seeking YHWH is given to David, and ultimately to the one who sought YHWH perfectly and received “all authority in heaven and on earth” (Matthew 28:18).
